"""
During the trading week containing 2026-05-13 (UTC+8), from 2026-05-13 through 2026-05-15 inclusive, what will be the highest daily Brent crude oil spot price, in USD per barrel, reported by FRED series DCOILBRENTEU?
"""

Your goal is to make a numeric prediction.

IMPORTANT: Your final answer MUST end with this exact format:

The number must be USD/bbl rounded to two decimals. Do not use any other final format. Do not refuse to make a prediction. You must make a clear prediction based on the best data currently available.

Brent crude averaged $117/b in April (highest since 2022), with a peak of $138/b; prices are forecast to average $106/b in May–June 2026, falling to $89/b in Q4 2026 and $79/b in 2027 as production resumes and inventories rebuild. Global oil inventories are projected to decline by 2.6 million b/d in 2026 (vs. prior forecast of 0.3 million b/d), with a rebound in 2027. OPEC’s spare capacity drops to 2.5 million b/d in 2027 after the UAE’s exit effective May 1, 2026. U.S. crude production remains flat at 13.6 million b/d in 2026, rising to 14.1 million b/d in 2027. Natural gas prices at Henry Hub fall to $3.50/MMBtu in 2026 and $3.18 in 2027 (down 4.4% and 11.5% from prior forecast), driven by rising production—up 1% in 2026 and 2% in 2027—led by Permian and Haynesville regions due to higher gas-to-oil ratios. U.S. LNG exports rise to 17 Bcf/d in 2026 and 18 Bcf/d in 2027, supported by new capacity from Golden Pass and Corpus Christi, though constrained by long lead times. Propane inventories hit record highs in late 2025 and remain above five-year averages through 2027, peaking at 112 million barrels in October 2026, driving U.S. net exports up 7% in 2026 and 10% in 2027 as Asia replaces Persian Gulf supply. U.S. electricity demand grows 1.3% in 2026 and 3.1% in 2027, led by commercial sector growth surpassing residential for the first time. Utility-scale solar generation forecast raised 1.4% due to revised capacity data. Coal’s share of electricity falls to 15% in 2027; natural gas remains dominant at 40%. Residential electricity prices rise 5% in 2026, with largest increases on the East Coast. U.S. CO₂ emissions decline slightly to 4.8 billion metric tons in 2026–2027. Global oil demand growth is revised down to 0.2 million b/d in 2026 (from 0.6 million b/d) due to reduced consumption in Asia, rebounding to 1.5 million b/d in 2027. Implied volatility for Brent crude averaged 78% in early 2026—highest since 2020—reflecting extreme market uncertainty. Strategic petroleum reserve releases and IEA coordinated draws partially offset supply shocks. Panama Canal bottlenecks and Targa facility maintenance temporarily constrain propane exports.", "url": "https://www.eia.gov/outlooks/steo/pdf/steo_full.pdf"}}

Crude Oil Prices: Brent - Europe (Series ID: DCOILBRENTEU), sourced from the U.S. Energy Information Administration, provides daily, non-seasonally adjusted prices in dollars per barrel from May 20, 1987, to May 1, 2026. Data is updated as of May 6, 2026. The dataset includes daily price observations with occasional missing values (denoted as “.”), primarily on weekends and holidays. Notable peaks include $27.35 on August 7, 1990, during the Gulf War oil shock, and $24.13 on August 3, 1990. rawPreview.text: Successful tool call result 2 with tool read_webpage: {"success": true, "data": {"content": "In April 2026, Brent crude oil spot prices surged past front-month futures prices by over $25/barrel, marking extreme backwardation not seen in years. This unprecedented spread reflects acute short-term supply tightness, primarily driven by disruptions to oil flows through the Strait of Hormuz, which forced buyers to scramble for immediate physical cargoes. Spot prices (Dated Brent), reflecting immediate delivery from North Sea terminals or CIF Rotterdam (including WTI cargoes since 2023), captured this urgency, while futures prices—traded for June delivery in April—lagged, as they priced in later supply. The front-month Brent futures contract, traded on the Intercontinental Exchange, remains the global benchmark, but its pricing mechanism excludes near-term physical constraints. Backwardation, typically narrow and positive under normal conditions, widened dramatically due to supply chain shocks, indicating a severe imbalance between current demand and available physical supply. The Brent basket includes BFOET grades (Brent, Forties, Oseberg, Ekofisk, Troll), with WTI now incorporated into pricing assessments since 2023 due to declining North Sea production. Price assessments are led by S&P Platts, Argus, and Reuters. The divergence underscores that spot markets better reflect immediate market stress than futures, which are forward-looking and less sensitive to near-term disruptions. rawPreview.text: Successful tool call result 2 with tool read_webpage: {"success": true, "data": {"content": "The May 2026 Short-Term Energy Outlook (STEO) by the EIA projects major disruptions to global oil markets due to the de facto closure of the Strait of Hormuz, with Middle Eastern crude production shut in by 10.5 million barrels per day (b/d) in April. The strait is expected to reopen gradually in late May, with full recovery delayed into late 2026, leading to a sharp global oil inventory draw of 2.6 million b/d in 2026 (vs. prior forecast of -0.3 million b/d). Brent crude averaged $117/b in April, peaking at $138/b; prices are forecast to average $106/b in May–June 2026, then decline to $89/b in Q4 2026 and $79/b in 2027. The UAE exited OPEC effective May 1, 2026, reducing OPEC spare capacity to 2.5 million b/d in 2027 (from 3.8 million b/d previously). U.S. propane inventories remain at record highs through 2026, pressuring prices and boosting exports to Asia as Persian Gulf supply is disrupted. U.S. LNG export capacity rose 0.9 Bcf/d in April, with additional capacity from Golden Pass and Corpus Christi; total exports forecast at 17.0 Bcf/d in 2026 and 18.2 Bcf/d in 2027. U.S. natural gas production rose 4% YoY in Q1 2026 to 120.2 Bcf/d, with revised forecasts up 1% for 2026 and 2% for 2027 due to higher gas-to-oil ratios in the Permian and Haynesville regions. Henry Hub price forecast lowered to $3.50/billion Btu in 2026 ($3.67 previously) and $3.18 in 2027 ($3.59 previously). U.S. electricity demand grows 1.3% in 2026 and 3.1% in 2027, led by commercial sector growth, which exceeds residential demand for the first time. Residential electricity prices rise 5% in 2026, with largest increases on the East Coast. Utility-scale solar generation forecast up 1.4% for 2026 due to revised capacity data. U.S. crude oil production holds steady at 13.6 million b/d in 2026, rising to 14.1 million b/d in 2027. U.S. CO₂ emissions decline slightly to 4,797 million metric tons in 2026 from 4,905 in 2025, then rise to 4,820 in 2027. GDP growth slows to 2.0% in both 2026 and 2027.", "url": "https://www.eia.gov/outlooks/steo/index.php", "page_links": [{"url": "https://twitter.com/EIAgov", "text": ""}, {"url": "https://www.facebook.com/eiagov", "text": ""}, {"url": "https://www.eia.gov/", "text": "U.S. Energy Information Administration - EIA - Independent Statistics and Analysis"}, {"url": "https://www.eia.gov/petroleum/", "text": "Petroleum & Other Liquids"}, {"url": "https://www.eia.gov/petroleum/gasdiesel/", "text": "Gasoline and Diesel Fuel Update"}, {"url": "https://www.eia.gov/petroleum/supply/weekly/", "text": "Weekly Petroleum Status Report"}, {"url": "https://www.eia.gov/naturalgas/", "text": "Natural Gas"}, {"url": "http://ir.eia.gov/ngs/ngs.html", "text": "Weekly Natural Gas Storage Report ›"}, {"url": "https://www.eia.gov/beta/naturalgas/weekly/supplement/", "text": "Weekly Natural Gas Storage Report Supplement"}, {"url": "https://www.eia.gov/electricity/", "text": "Electricity"}, {"url": "https://www.eia.gov/electricity/monthly/", "text": "Electric Power Monthly"}, {"url": "https://www.eia.gov/electricity/data/browser/", "text": "Electricity Data Browser"}, {"url": "https://www.eia.gov/consumption/", "text": "Consumption & Efficiency"}, {"url": "https://www.eia.gov/consumption/residential/", "text": "Residential Energy Consumption Survey (RECS)"}, {"url": "https://www.eia.gov/consumption/commercial/", "text": "Commercial Buildings Energy Consumption Survey (CBECS)"}]}}

President Trump claimed the ceasefire held despite US strikes on Iranian military targets after Iranian forces attacked three US destroyers—though officials denied seeking escalation. The Strait of Hormuz, effectively closed since late February, continues to disrupt global crude flows, with the International Energy Agency estimating 14 million barrels per day removed from supply. Saudi Aramco CEO Amin Nasser warned of weekly supply losses of up to 100 million barrels, with normalization potentially delayed until next year. Earlier in the week, prices had risen above $107 as Trump dismissed Iran’s peace proposal as “totally unacceptable,” fueling fears of prolonged disruption. Iran demanded an end to the US naval blockade and control over Hormuz traffic, while limited tanker transit resumed from UAE, Saudi Arabia, and Qatar—still far below pre-conflict levels. Drone attacks near Qatar and missile interceptions in UAE and Kuwait underscored persistent security risks. Brent crude oil prices surged to $107.16 per barrel on May 12, 2026, up 2.83% daily, 7.85% monthly, and 60.83% year-over-year, driven by escalating tensions between the U.S. and Iran. Data is updated as of May 6, 2026. The dataset includes daily price observations with occasional missing values (denoted as “.”), primarily on weekends and holidays. Notable peaks include $27.35 on August 7, 1990, during the Gulf War oil shock, and $24.13 on August 3, 1990. Prices dropped sharply in late 1986–early 1987 (to $13.18 in September 1988) and again in mid-1990 (to $14.68 in June 1990). The series shows high volatility during geopolitical events and supply disruptions. The data is used for energy market analysis and economic forecasting. No seasonal adjustments are applied. The dataset is publicly accessible via the FRED database with explanatory notes available through the EIA website."}}

Webpage content:
The May 2026 Short-Term Energy Outlook (STEO) by the EIA projects major disruptions to global oil markets due to the de facto closure of the Strait of Hormuz, with Middle Eastern crude production shut in by 10.5 million barrels per day (b/d) in April. The strait is expected to reopen gradually in late May, with full recovery delayed into late 2026, leading to a sharp global oil inventory draw of 2.6 million b/d in 2026 (vs. prior forecast of -0.3 million b/d). Brent crude averaged $117/b in April, peaking at $138/b; prices are forecast to average $106/b in May–June 2026, then decline to $89/b in Q4 2026 and $79/b in 2027. The UAE exited OPEC effective May 1, 2026, reducing OPEC spare capacity to 2.5 million b/d in 2027 (from 3.8 million b/d previously). U.S. propane inventories remain at record highs through 2026, pressuring prices and boosting exports to Asia as Persian Gulf supply is disrupted. U.S. LNG export capacity rose 0.9 Bcf/d in April, with additional capacity from Golden Pass and Corpus Christi; total exports forecast at 17.0 Bcf/d in 2026 and 18.2 Bcf/d in 2027. U.S. natural gas production rose 4% YoY in Q1 2026 to 120.2 Bcf/d, with revised forecasts up 1% for 2026 and 2% for 2027 due to higher gas-to-oil ratios in the Permian and Haynesville regions. Henry Hub price forecast lowered to $3.50/billion Btu in 2026 ($3.67 previously) and $3.18 in 2027 ($3.59 previously). U.S. electricity demand grows 1.3% in 2026 and 3.1% in 2027, led by commercial sector growth, which exceeds residential demand for the first time. Residential electricity prices rise 5% in 2026, with largest increases on the East Coast. Utility-scale solar generation forecast up 1.4% for 2026 due to revised capacity data. U.S. crude oil production holds steady at 13.6 million b/d in 2026, rising to 14.1 million b/d in 2027. U.S. CO₂ emissions decline slightly to 4,797 million metric tons in 2026 from 4,905 in 2025, then rise to 4,820 in 2027. GDP growth slows to 2.0% in both 2026 and 2027."}}

Webpage content:
Brent crude oil prices surged to $107.16 per barrel on May 12, 2026, up 2.83% daily, 7.85% monthly, and 60.83% year-over-year, driven by escalating tensions between the U.S. and Iran. President Trump dismissed Iran’s latest peace proposal, declaring the ceasefire “on massive life support,” raising fears of prolonged closure of the Strait of Hormuz—a critical global oil transit route. Iran demanded an end to the U.S. naval blockade and sanctions while seeking control over maritime traffic. Saudi Aramco CEO Amin Nasser warned the market is losing ~100 million barrels of supply weekly, with normalization potentially delayed until 2027. Drone attacks near Qatar and interceptions in the UAE and Kuwait underscore ongoing regional insecurity, while Israel’s Netanyahu declared the Iran conflict “not over.” Limited tanker traffic has resumed but remains far below pre-crisis levels. Brent, a key global benchmark for light sweet crude from the North Sea, is priced via CFDs and OTC instruments, not official benchmarks. Forecasts project Brent at $103.40 by end-Q2 2026 and $116.69 in 12 months. Historical peak was $147.50 in July 2008. Rising prices are fueling inflation concerns and disrupting global energy flows, including LNG and refined fuels. Other energy commodities like gasoline and heating oil also posted strong gains, while natural gas and coal declined. Geopolitical risk remains the dominant driver of oil markets."}}

forecastPayload.rationale: The best same-source anchor is EIA’s own May STEO average of 106 USD/b for May–June, while live market proxies immediately before the window show Brent already rebounding to about 107. That combination supports a target-window high a bit above 107 but well below the late-April panic highs, yielding 108.74 USD/bbl as the most plausible maximum daily DCOILBRENTEU observation for 2026-05-13 to 2026-05-15.
keyEvidenceItems: The official EIA daily spot page for Brent - Europe (the source chain behind FRED DCOILBRENTEU) showed Brent already at 118.26 USD/bbl on 2026-05-01 after late-April spikes above 124.
EIA’s May 2026 STEO projected Brent spot prices to average 106 USD/b in May–June 2026, implying mid-May levels should be around the low 100s rather than back at late-April extremes.
Contemporaneous market proxies showed Brent rebounding from about 101 USD/bbl on 2026-05-08 to 107.16 USD/bbl on 2026-05-12 as ceasefire hopes faded and Hormuz disruption fears resurfaced.

A 2026-05-11 Barchart report showed WTI at 101.26 USD/bbl with strongly bullish supply-shock fundamentals, consistent with Brent holding a several-dollar premium and keeping the weekly Brent high above the May-June average.
counterEvidenceItems: An OilPrice headline in the search results suggested crude could fall back below $100 if Trump signaled the Iran war might be ending, so de-escalation risk is real.

The latest directly observed EIA daily Brent-Europe spot value available in-tool was still 118.26 on 2026-05-01, leaving a gap between the official series history and the target window that must be bridged with proxies.
openConcerns: Whether FRED/EIA Brent-Europe spot tracks the quoted Brent futures/CFD level nearly one-for-one during this period, or with a meaningful basis difference.
Whether headlines during 2026-05-13 to 2026-05-15 produce a fresh geopolitical spike above 110, or instead a rapid de-escalation back toward 100.
temporalNotes: Official EIA daily spot observations retrieved in-tool were available through 2026-05-01 on a page released 2026-05-06; target-window values were not yet directly visible at checkpoint time.
Latest proxy market evidence used for trajectory was dated 2026-05-11 to 2026-05-12, immediately before the forecast window.

sourceObservations.title: Galaxy final prediction
sourceObservations.summary: The question asks for the highest daily Brent crude oil spot price, in the exact FRED/EIA series DCOILBRENTEU, during 2026-05-13 through 2026-05-15. The run established that the official series is an EIA-sourced daily Brent-Europe spot measure published through FRED, but direct target-window observations were not yet visible; the latest official data only reached 2026-05-01. To bridge that gap, the forecaster combined the official series history and EIA’s May 2026 STEO with near-real-time Brent and crude market reporting. What emerged was a picture of extreme late-April spikes fading toward an EIA-expected May average near 106, followed by a renewed rebound to roughly 107 immediately before the target window as ceasefire hopes weakened. That mix supported forecasting a weekly high slightly above 107, but not another late-April panic extreme.
